steps.title: Identify the list structure and complexity
steps.content: The sentence states “three main factors—namely” and then lists them. The second and third items contain internal commas: “their velocity upon entering Earth's mesosphere, with higher-velocity meteoroids releasing more energy” and “atmospheric fluctuations, such as changes in humidity.” This makes the list complex.

steps.title: Apply the complex-list rule
steps.content: When listing items and at least one item contains internal punctuation (commas), Standard English uses semicolons to separate the main items for clarity: “Item 1; Item 2; and Item 3.” The comma (B) would blur the boundaries between factors because commas appear inside the second and third factors. The em dash (A) wrongly suggests elaboration rather than listing. The period (D) would incorrectly end the sentence. That matches choice C — C is correct.
